entry.title: Effective Start Date Validation in Payment Information - SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Introduction :

Every time after the payroll run, One of my Clients faced a recurring issue and this recurring issue was causing last minute hassles and inconvenience to the Business. The Issue is the maintenance of Bank Details in the Payment Information Portlet mid of the month by the HR Team as they don't notice the effective start date which by defaults sets to today. Maintenance of mid-month Bank details will impact all the payroll reports, and the reports will show inconsistent values , which will then impact reconciliation and so on..

How a simple and effective Business Rule solved this challenge?

I noticed this issue become so common over the days and as a consultant it's my core duty to understand and resolve the business pain point. So I configured a simple and effective rule which will solve this recurring issue and any issues that follow by.

The Business Rule :

Base Object : Payment Information

Purpose : Evaluate / Validate

This simple rule here does the magic of validating the effective start and throwing error message when maintained mid of the month. There's a little catch and purpose behind using the previous effective validation here , the reason is explained below

We don't have an option in Business Rule for MDF's to have it triggered only for certain scenarios which means this will also trigger in the new hire UI. Imagine an employee joining mid of the month 03rd May 2026 , the validation kicks and throws error , ID is skipped and issue occurs.

To avoid this rule being triggered for the new hires , Previous Effective dated record is not equal to null condition is added in the IF condition.

Conclusion

This simple validation rule effectively prevents mid-month changes to bank details, eliminating inconsistencies in payroll reports and reducing reconciliation issues.

It also highlights an important lesson:
Every validation or enhancement should be carefully designed with edge cases in mind and thoroughly tested across all scenarios.

entry.title: Latest People Profile Enablement: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog explains the points to be noted, key configurations to be done, and behaviours observed during the migration from the legacy People Profile to the latest People Profile.

Legacy People Profile to Latest People Profile Migration

Migration process:

To migrate configurations from the Legacy People Profile to the Latest People Profile: 

  • Navigate to Company System and Logo Settings >> Select “Migrate Legacy People Profile Configurations” >> Run the migration job  

With the 1H 2026 release, the Latest People Profile is automatically enabled, and the migration job is executed by default. However, administrators can still trigger the job manually if required. This change cannot be reverted to the Legacy People Profile.

Card Arrangement After Migration:

After the migration job is completed, both predefined and custom cards are reorganized into structured categories.

Predefined category:

Custom Category:

  • Custom blocks such as Live Profile Background Information, MDF Information, User Information, and Notes are moved to the Full Profile as custom cards.
  • These cards are organized into custom categories, keeping the same structure as in the old (legacy) profile layout.

System Limitations in Latest People Profile :

  • Maximum 20 custom categories allowed  
  • Maximum 30 cards per category  

Setting Category Access Control in Latest People Profile:

By default, the latest People Profile categories are visible to everyone. To restrict visibility based on roles, change the access to Role-Based Permissions.

Edit each category and configure the category access control.

 

Aligning Employee View Permissions Between Legacy and Latest People Profile

Navigate to Manage Permission Roles → Edit Role → Employee Views.

Under Employee Views, two categories are available:

  • People Profile Categories – used for the latest People Profile
  • Employee View Sections (only for Legacy People Profile) – used for the legacy People Profile

Refer to the permissions defined under Employee View Sections (only for Legacy People Profile) and provide similar permissions in People Profile Categories for each role.

You can also generate a report to review the list of roles and their existing legacy Employee View permissions and use it as a reference.

Set Header Fields in Latest People Profile:

During migration from legacy to the latest People Profile, header fields will not be migrated. They must be configured again in Configure Latest People Profile → General Settings → Configure Header Fields.

Common Issues and Behaviours in Latest People Profile Configuration:

  1. If there are duplicate sections or duplicate objects in the legacy People Profile during migration to the latest People Profile, only the first occurrence is considered, and the subsequent ones will not be displayed.
  2. Non-secured objects will not be displayed in the latest People Profile. For example, Advances may initially not be visible, so ensure the security for both Non-Recurring Payments and Advances objects is set to “Yes” in Configure Object Definitions.
  3. If the default screen in Configure Object Definition for objects to be displayed in the Employee Profile is not set, then even if the object permission is not granted to the employee, the card will still be displayed in their profile with an error. So, make sure to set it.
  4. If the compensation pie chart is not displayed in the employee’s Profile Compensation Information, it is because the employee does not have permission for the Pay Component Group.
  5. Recurring Deduction – even if permission is not granted, the card is displayed when there is no data. However, once data is entered, the card disappears, but there is no functional impact.
  6. If the Compensation Statement card is not visible in the Employee Profile, ensure that the necessary permission is granted for that in employee data.
  7. If any custom objects are set inside standard sections like Job Information, Compensation Information, etc., in the legacy People Profile, they will disappear during the latest People Profile migration and need to be set again in the relevant categories in the latest People Profile.
  8. In the legacy People Profile, the Org Chart was available under General Information. In the latest People Profile, the Org Chart is now available under Actions.

Conclusion

By May 15,16, 2026, the Latest People Profile will be automatically enabled in production. Therefore, ensure that all necessary configurations are completed, and the required permissions are granted.

Make sure to review legacy configurations and migrate any required settings to the latest People Profile categories. Validate role-based permissions to avoid missing or restricted visibility of cards and sections. Also, test the profile view to ensure all relevant data is displayed correctly after migration.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: Unlocking Next-Level Innovation to AI with SAP Business AI Platform
entry.content:
*Updated on May 15 with SAP Business AI Platform

Hello SAP Community!

Mark your calendars – SAP Sapphire 2026 is here, and it's set to redefine what's possible. This year, the conversation isn’t just about digital transformation. The focus is on mastering your platform journey to drive continuous innovation, achieve measurable business outcomes, and on how organizations should leverage artificial intelligence. It’s going to be a fantastic opportunity to learn, share together, and of course, I’m especially looking forward to celebrating with everyone at the afterparty. I just found out there’s hazelnut chocolate fruit waffles at Magic Kingdom, so I’m extra excited!

From AI-powered development and intelligent automation to seamless integration and strategic platform management, SAP’s Business AI platform (SAP BAIP) stands as the essential foundation. Its unique differentiation lies in deeply embedding AI across your operations while preserving the invaluable data and business application context, thereby turning challenges into tangible opportunities. Sapphire 2026 will be the pivotal event to launch SAP’s strategic vision, highlighting how AI innovations are integral to the enterprises of tomorrow.

 

The Mandate: Connecting Everything, Across the Entire Landscape

In an era when disconnected landscapes continue to hinder progress, SAP’s customers have consistently shown that the platform's true power lies in end-to-end connectivity. Whether it's your CHRO leveraging SAP SuccessFactors employee data with procurement data for newly onboarding employees, or your CFO optimizing financials with SAP S/4HANA together with external third-party benchmark data, or your CPO driving procurement excellence by keeping warehouse data up to date from supply chain partners, the challenge remains: how do you connect these vital functions, including between the AI agents, to move beyond implementation to true business transformation?

This year, you’ll see how SAP Business AI platform serves as the critical foundation for SAP’s business applications, making this connectivity a reality. It's designed to streamline operations, foster innovation, and enable real-time analysis of insights across your entire business landscape.

 

Spotlight on Transformation: Breakthrough Use Cases for a Connected Future

This Sapphire, we're particularly excited to unveil new use cases that exemplify the SAP Business AI platform’s power to address today's most pressing business challenges.

 

1. Integrate HR data and processes with government and third‑party systems

  • The Challenge: Navigating complex and evolving HR regulations, increasing demands for efficiency in HR service delivery, and managing diverse workforces effectively.
  • How the SAP BAIP helps: By integrating HR data in SAP SuccessFactors (or elsewhere) and processes with government and third-party systems through SAP Integration Suite, organizations can ensure compliance with global and local regulations, automate processes, and streamline interactions with third-parties. This connected process frees the HR department from administrative burdens, creating streamlined employee experiences and compliant data.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Proving the business value of standardizing on SAP Integration Suite | BTP2350: Hear how teams quantify business value after standardizing on SAP Integration Suite: before-and-after metrics, faster time to integrate, fewer incidents, lower operating effort, and readiness for a mixed SAP and third-party software landscape. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • PPG Industries: Building an AI-ready, clean core integration foundation | BTP2638: See how APIs, events, and cloud integration reduce complexity, improve visibility, and establish an AI-ready foundation for future innovation across SAP and third-party systems. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Building a foundation for AI with an integrated core HR system | HCM1742: Learn how a connected approach to core HR, payroll, workforce management, and HR service delivery can help you streamline your people and business processes while enabling AI readiness. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

2. Drive profitable growth with secure, personalized digital experiences

  • The Challenge: Organizations struggle with complex processes and fragmented access to customer data, facing increasing competition and rising customer expectations. Data silos often lead to slow and reactive decision-making, impacting customer satisfaction and growth.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (SAP CIAM) protects sensitive customer data and meets global compliance requirements. By consolidating and integrating customer data across various systems with SAP’s platform, it enables the creation of secure, personalized digital experiences that overcome data silos. This supports dynamic and tailored customer interactions, enhancing customer acquisition and retention.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Standardize, secure, and scale with core platform capabilities | BTP1315: Learn how application lifecycle management, security, and interoperability unlock SAP’s Platform to drive agility and growth. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Invisible security: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management | BTP1316: Discover how SAP Customer Identity and Access Management accelerates your business by harnessing AI-assisted risk analysis to stop threats in real time, protecting customers behind the scenes without disrupting experiences. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Secure by design: How SAP is redefining agent execution | BTP2641: Learn how SAP is developing the secure execution layer, combining runtime containment with the governance of AI-enabled data fabric solutions from SAP to define production-ready agentic AI.
      🗓️🗓ADD NOW

 

3. Leverage AI Capabilities in ABAP Cloud Development

  • The Challenge: Developers struggle to balance rapid delivery with maintaining code standards, while traditional coding, debugging, and testing consume a significant portion of their time. Complex legacy code also slows productivity.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Leveraging AI capabilities directly within ABAP Cloud Development, using tools like SAP Joule for Developers, can dramatically increase developer productivity. AI assists with code generation, ensures code quality, and helps your development teams understand complex systems, freeing them to focus on innovation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Agentic ABAP with SAP Joule for Developers: Build faster, migrate smarter | JOU1428: Find out how AI agents with ABAP simplify migration, speed up coding, and increase productivity, even in older versions of SAP S/4HANA. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • ABAP Cloud: Entering the new era of ABAP development | BTP2107: The ABAP Cloud development model, agentic development experience in VS Code, the SAP Joule for Developers add-on, and agentic custom code migration join forces for the next era of growth in ABAP development tools. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Introduction to ABAP Cloud supported by generative AI | BTP1429: Learn what ABAP Cloud is, get insights into the latest technology advancements, and see how generative AI boosts your adoption of ABAP Cloud. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

4. Modernize On-Premise Integration to Cloud with PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite Migration

  • The Challenge: Organizations still using SAP Process Integration/Process Orchestration (SAP PI/PO) are facing the impending need to modernize the integration methods. With a complex integration landscape comprising multiple integration solutions, not only does it add complexity, but it also creates greater technical debt.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Migrating from SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite provides a modern, cloud-native platform that reduces Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) across the integration landscape. This transition can cut the time to set up new interfaces and unlock significant annual cost savings. It's a strategic move that not only streamlines operations but also provides the scalable, agile integration backbone essential for future AI-driven cloud transformation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Modernize your strategy: Migrating from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite | BTP1360: Learn how the suite enables smooth, scalable, and intelligent integrations across cloud and hybrid landscapes. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Under Armour: Enabling real-time commerce with SAP Integration Suite | BTP2138: Under Armour is consolidating MuleSoft, Boomi, and SAP Process Integration and SAP Process Orchestration into SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Modernize from SAP Process Integration and SAP Process Orchestration | BTP2100: Learn about migration approaches, coexistence options, and governance—plus the benefits customers see after moving, such as faster change cycles and better reliability and monitoring.
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

Mastering Your SAP Business AI Platform Journey: Measure, Optimize, and Guide


To truly maximize your investment in SAP BAIP, it’s crucial to understand where your organization currently stands and how to strategically advance. Sapphire 2026 will highlight the tools and methodologies designed to guide this journey, available on SAP Discovery Center: 

  • SAP Discovery Center: As your central hub for knowledge and hands-on learning, the Discovery Center continues to evolve. Discover, evaluate, and adopt SAP’s Platform and AI to create tailored business solutions and accelerate your platform adoption.

  • Maturity Assessment: Introduced at last year’s SAP TechEd, this comprehensive technical evaluation transforms uncertainty into actionable insights. In just 10-15 minutes, you can get a precise understanding of your organization's platform maturity across five levels, from "Getting Started" to "Center of Excellence." It provides a clear roadmap, justifies further investments, and uncovers hidden efficiencies and innovation opportunities.

We have since expanded the portfolio with new Line of Business AI assessments for domain-specific insights, and an AI Maturity Calculator is in development with IDC that not only assesses your platform AI readiness but also benchmarks you against your industry peers.

  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Your platform journey: Maturity assessment and Adoption Best Practices | BTP1549: Explore the new maturity assessment that benchmarks your current state and uncovers optimization opportunities, paired with the proven platform adoption framework for SAP BTP that guides implementation.  
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • BTP101: Comprehensive Overview of SAP’s Platform — Foundations, Development, Integration, and Governance | ASUG105: This intensive 6-hour workshop provides a complete foundational overview of SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and its core capabilities. Participants will explore the BTP ecosystem, from architecture and development frameworks to enterprise integration and data management solutions.
      🗓️ADD NOW

Your Strategic Next Steps at Sapphire 2026: Attend Must-Attend Sessions:

This Sapphire, don't just attend – Seize this opportunity to proactively empower your organization by understanding the strategic future of AI. In addition to use-case specific recommendations, I’ve identified essential sessions to deepen your strategic understanding.

Secure your spot: add these pivotal sessions to your agenda to fully grasp SAP’s AI and platform vision for enterprise innovation.

  • Scaling AI in cloud ERP with SAP's platform | BTP1343: Learn how the AI platform helps your AI in cloud ERP move from experiments to real, repeatable results. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Using autonomous finance to jump-start an intelligent enterprise | BTP1345: Hear how SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build solutions, and the SAP Business Data Cloud solution offer the framework to orchestrate cross-enterprise processes, helping them steer the corporate growth plan. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • SAP's AI platform: What's new, what's next, and why it matters | BTP1448: Get a tour of the latest innovations, hands‑on tools for developers and business users, and an early preview of upcoming capabilities shaping the future of AI on the platform. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Design the future of enterprise AI agents with Joule Studio | BTP1449: Discover how Joule Studio enables teams to shape the next era of intelligent enterprises. With an intuitive no‑code experience, create secure, context‑aware AI agents that plan, reason, and act across SAP solutions and beyond.
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • From idea to impact: Orchestrating across the AI agent lifecycle | BTP1450: Get a tour of the latest innovations, hands‑on tools for developers and business users, and an early preview of upcoming capabilities shaping the future of AI on the platform. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • The enterprise path to agentic AI | BTP1454: Discover how Joule Studio creates AI solutions from extensions to agentic apps. SAP’s strategy delivers an enterprise-grade intent-to-execution platform that is purpose-built for SAP customers. 
    🗓️ADD NOW
  • Unlock platform value with AI at Cardinal Health and Johnson Controls | BTP1959: Hear how Cardinal Health Inc. and Johnson Controls International plc unlock platform value with AI-enabled application development, integration, automation, and AI services.
    🗓️ADD NOW

See you there!

Sapphire 2026 is your opportunity to discover how SAP Business AI Platform can propel your organization forward to AI adoption, helping you become a truly connected enterprise with governance, ready for whatever the future holds. If you’re at Sapphire, please come see me at booth 152 – BTP1958 and let’s chat!

entry.title: Generating Documents via a Link in Custom MDF Objects in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Business Case & Problem Statement

In many SAP SuccessFactors implementations, HR teams rely on a variety of employee-facing documents — offer letters, confirmation letters, salary certificates, experience letters, and more. Traditionally, generating these documents requires navigating multiple screens: locating the employee profile, accessing the Document Generation module, selecting the appropriate template, and downloading the result. This multi-step process is not only time-consuming but also error-prone, especially when the document context is tied to a specific business record stored in a custom Metadata Framework (MDF) object.

The challenge becomes even more pronounced in scenarios where:

  • HR business processes are captured in custom MDF objects (e.g., a probation review record, a training completion record, or a custom agreement tracker).
  • The document to be generated depends on the type of record selected in the MDF object (e.g., different letter types mapped via a picklist).
  • HR administrators want a single-click experience directly from the MDF object record, without switching contexts or re-entering data.

The Solution

SAP SuccessFactors provides a powerful combination of Document Generation, the Metadata Framework, Picklist Center, and Configuration UI that — when wired together correctly — enables HR teams to generate and download a PDF document directly from a link embedded in a custom MDF object screen. No additional navigation. No re-entry of data. One click.

This blog walks through the complete configuration required to achieve this. It is intended for SAP SuccessFactors consultants and HR system administrators who are comfortable working in Admin Center and the Metadata Framework.

Key Business Benefits

  • Reduced Process Time: HR administrators can generate the correct document in a single click directly from the MDF record, eliminating context-switching and manual lookups.
  • Dynamic Template Selection: By linking the picklist external code to the Document Template ID, the system automatically selects the right template based on the document type chosen in the MDF record — no hardcoding required.
  • Scalability: Adding new document types is as simple as adding a new picklist value and a new template — no changes to the MDF object definition or UI configuration are needed.
  • Improved Data Accuracy: Token-based data population pulls live data directly from the system of record, ensuring documents always reflect accurate, up-to-date information without manual intervention.

Solution Architecture Overview

The configuration involves five interdependent components that must be set up in sequence:

  • Document Generation Template — the Word-based template with token placeholders that will be merged into the final PDF.
  • Picklist with External Codes — a picklist whose external code values match the Document Template IDs, acting as the bridge between user selection and template resolution.
  • Custom MDF Object — the business record object with a Picklist field (to select document type) and a String field (to hold the generation link).
  • Configuration UI with a Link — the UI screen for the MDF object, enhanced with a dynamic link element that triggers document generation with the correct token values injected into the URL.
  • Business Rule — an optional but recommended rule to automate link population upon save, ensuring the link is always in sync with the selected document type.

Let’s walk through each step in detail.

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Create Document Generation Template

  • Template Creation Process: Navigate to Admin Center > Document Generation - Manage Document Template and click "Create New"
  • Template Configuration Settings: Select "Document Generation Template" as the category and configure template content with placeholders using [[tokenName]] syntax
  • Document ID Assignment: Assign a unique Document ID to your template - this will be crucial for the picklist configuration in the next step

Step 2: Configure Picklist with External Code

  • Picklist Creation Steps: Go to Admin Center > Picklist Center and create a new picklist for document types
  • External Code Configuration Matching Document ID: Set the external code of your picklist values to match exactly with the Document ID from Step 1 - this creates the connection between your MDF object and the document template

Step 3: Create Custom MDF Object with Required Fields

Field 1: Document Type Field

  • Document Type Field Configuration: In Configure Object Definitions, create a custom MDF object and add a field for document type
  • Picklist Assignment to Document Type Field: Set the data type as "Picklist" and assign the picklist created in Step 2 as the Valid Values Source

Field 2: String Field(Generate Document)

  • String Field Creation: Add a second custom field with data type "String" to capture additional information needed for document generation
  • Field Properties and Data Type Settings: Configure field length, validation rules, and visibility settings as per business requirements

 

Step 4: Create UI and Configure Link with Token Mapping

  • UI Creation Process: Navigate to Admin Center > Manage Configuration UI and create a new UI screen for your custom MDF object
  • Link Addition to UI: Use the "Add Link" functionality in the UI configuration to add a document generation link
  • Token Insertion Methodology: Configure the link URL with tokens that will dynamically populate values from your MDF object fields.

 

Step 5: Create Business Rule with Instance Configuration

Generate Document and Download PDF

  • Document Generation Link Activation: After selecting values in your custom MDF object and saving the record, click on the "Generate Document" link

Summary & Final Thoughts

By combining SAP SuccessFactors’ Document Generation module with a custom MDF object, a Picklist with aligned external codes, a Configuration UI link, and an optional Business Rule, you can deliver a seamless one-click document generation experience to your HR team — all without any external integrations.

The key insight in this configuration is the use of the picklist’s external code as the bridge between the user’s document type selection and the Document Template ID. This elegant mapping makes the solution both flexible and scalable — new document types can be supported simply by adding a new picklist entry and a matching template, with zero changes to the object definition or UI.

This pattern can be applied to a wide range of HR use cases: probation confirmation letters, training completion certificates, employment verification letters, custom agreement forms, and more. Once the framework is in place, rolling out additional document types becomes a configuration-only activity.

Have questions or a variation of this use case? Feel free to share in the comments below. Happy configuring!

entry.title: Future-Dated Records on Terminated Employees: 1H 2026 Finally Closes a Long-Standing Gap.
entry.content:
If you've worked in Employee Central long enough, you know this pattern. Someone gets terminated, but a future-dated promotion, comp change, or position move is sitting in their record. Payroll picks it up. Replication chokes. Someone files a ticket Monday morning. Repeat.

Customers have been raising this for years. The 1H 2026 release finally addresses it through enhancement ECT-260432, driven by Customer Idea 249640.

What Actually Changes

When you terminate an employee, the system now automatically deletes any future-dated Job Information and Compensation Information records dated after the termination date. Related Position Management data follows the same rule. Time Off records are not covered, so keep your Termination End Handling process running for those.

The cleanup runs whether the termination flows through the UI (Latest or Legacy People Profile, Action Search, or Manage My Team), an import, or an OData API call. Every auto-deleted record lands in the audit logs, so you don't lose visibility.

There's also a smaller related improvement. If an admin tries to create a data change record on an already-terminated employee, the system now warns that the target user is inactive but still allows the save, because there are legitimate cases for it.

The Scenarios This Finally Puts to Rest

The mid-cycle resignation. A promotion approved last month, effective next month. The employee resigns in between. Until now, that future record sat there, payroll picked it up, replication choked, someone wrote a P1 ticket on a Monday morning.

The merit cycle ghost. April 1 comp updates pushed across the org. One employee resigns mid-March. Until now, the comp record stayed put, finance reports overstated forward salary spend, and someone wrote a manual reconciliation script.

The fiscal-year position move. A position change scheduled for the new fiscal year. Employee leaves before it kicks in. Until now, ghost position data confused the org chart and your S/4HANA replication target until the next data audit caught it.

If you've lived through any of these, you know none of them are exotic edge cases. They're Tuesday.

Two Things to Know Before You Turn It On

1. The default depends on the event reason type. For non-status-changing event reasons, the cleanup is automatically enabled and no action is required.

For status-changing event reasons, the "Delete Future-Dated Records After Terminate Action" toggle in Manage Organization, Pay and Job Structures → Event Reason defaults to No.

To extend cleanup to those, flip it to Yes per event reason.

Audit your event reason configuration before going live. That's the only way to make sure you actually catch every case relevant to your org. Note: this field can't be included in reports, so the audit has to be done in the configuration UI.

2. It does not apply to changes made through the History UI. If your admins use History UI to back-date or correct termination records, future-dated records will not be touched.

You'll still need a manual sweep for those flows. Worth flagging to your support team before the release lands.

Why This One Matters

This won't show up in any Joule launch video. There's no demo with sparkles. But for anyone who has spent a Monday morning chasing down a payroll replication failure caused by a comp record on someone who left two weeks ago, this is the kind of fix that quietly buys back weekends.

Audit your event reasons. Update your termination runbook. And give your operations team the news.

entry.title: 08.SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation - Data Migration Key areas and Framework
entry.content:
This is part of an ongoing blog series on SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation. In this post, we are going to talk about the key areas of the Data Migration workstream. Data migration is one of the most complex workstreams in the SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation project. It is often the primary factor that determines whether an implementation is a resounding success or a mess.

I have covered all critical areas at a high level but will write a detailed blog on the critical section with all details.

 

 

 

1) Data Migration Scope : When initiating the Data Migration workstream, the highest priority is to finalize the Data Migration Scope. This is a critical foundational step, as the defined scope directly influences migration complexity, effort, resource requirements, sequencing, and the overall project timeline.

Key areas that play a critical role in scope definition include:

  • Existing system architecture and source systems for data
  • Modules and business processes in scope
  • Data sources and data ownership
  • Historical data retention and migration requirements
  • Compliance, audit, and regulatory considerations
  • Reporting and analytics requirements
  • Data quality and data volume considerations
  • Integrations and downstream dependencies
  • Security and access requirements

2) Data Migration Tools :Identifying the right migration tool is a critical success factor for the Data Migration workstream. The selection of the migration approach and tooling largely depends on the source systems, data structure, transformation requirements, data volume, and overall integration landscape.

Based on the Customer’s existing systems and migration requirements, the project team must evaluate and select the most appropriate migration tools and frameworks. The chosen toolset should support efficient extraction, transformation, validation, and loading of data while ensuring data quality, traceability, and compliance requirements are met.

SAP provides Info Porter Tools to migrate Foundation, Org and Employee data  from the below systems to SF EC. This tool works really great for data migration loads.

  • SAP S/4HANA HCM
  • SAP ERP HCM

3) Data Migration Team - SI Skillset and Customer Resources availability: Data Migration requires strong commitment and dedicated participation from both the Customer and the System Integrator (SI). Data migration activities, such as data mapping, data validation, data cleansing, reconciliation, and testing, are highly business-driven and require deep knowledge of the Customer’s existing systems, data, and processes.

At the same time, it is equally important to ensure the SI provides experienced Data Migration consultants who can offer the right guidance, proven methodologies, and strong delivery ownership.

4) Data Migration Requirements and Mapping workbook ownership: Capturing Data Migration requirements is one of the first and most critical activities during the Realization phase. A well-defined migration requirement and mapping framework establishes the foundation for successful data transformation, validation, and loading activities throughout the project lifecycle.

Defining migration requirements requires close coordination and collaboration among multiple stakeholders, including:

  • SuccessFactors (SF) Functional Resources
  • Data Migration (DM)  Team

Since migration requirements evolve throughout the project, it is important to clearly define ownership, governance, and update processes for the Data Migration Mapping Workbook from the beginning.

5) Data Migration Build and Unit Testing:  Identifying the right team for the Data Migration workstream is critical to the success of the overall implementation. The Data Migration team must have a strong understanding of the SuccessFactors (SF) Data Model, object relationships, data dependencies, and migration sequencing requirements.

At a minimum, key resources assigned to the project should have prior experience in SuccessFactors Data Migration and hands-on knowledge of:

  • SuccessFactors Employee Central data structures
  • Foundation Objects and MDF Objects
  • Effective-dated data handling
  • Load sequencing and dependency management
  • SAP migration tools and templates
  • Data transformation and validation techniques

6) Mock Cycles Planning:  Because Data Migration is one of the most complex and risk-prone workstreams, ne of the key success factors for migration readiness is defining the appropriate number of mock migration cycles before the Production load. More mocks generally provide higher-quality data, but they also increase project timelines. The required number depends on customer complexity, global footprint, and the project deployment approach. I strongly recommend 3 mock cycles with a full load prior to the production load

The number of mock cycles should be determined based on several factors, including:

  • Customer complexity
  • Global footprint and localization requirements
  • Number of countries/business units in scope
  • Volume of historical data
  • Data quality maturity of legacy systems
  • Complexity of integrations and downstream systems
  • Deployment and rollout approach
  • Compliance and reporting requirements

7) Instance Strategy: A dedicated SuccessFactors (SF) instance is required to migrate data, and the SF instance cannot be used for any other purpose during data loads. At the same time, ensuring that the source system (e.g., SAP S/4HANA) is available and that production data has been copied is critical. This must be properly planned as part of the instance strategy for each mock cycle, including which systems will be used. If necessary, an additional SF instance should be provided.

8)Entry and exit criteria for each mock:  For each mock load, we must clearly define the entry criteria required to initiate the exercise, as well as the exit criteria that determine successful completion. As part of the data migration workstream, each activity should also be assigned defined entry criteria and timelines. Without these clearly established controls, there is a high risk of delays and misalignment across dependent activities.

9)Data Scrambling: Protecting sensitive data and coming up with the right data scrambling approach is key. We typically do not migrate real email addresses, national IDs, compensation details, or payment details during Mock loads. Depending on the usage of mock load data, we may include or exclude sensitive data.

If sensitive data is not required during mock loads, we scramble data in the source system or in SuccessFactors before granting user’s access. Partner tools are available to support data scrambling, and some customers also have built-in tools to do so.

 10) Data  Validation Tools and Approach: Data validation requires significant effort. Identifying the right tools—both internal and external—that enable business teams to continuously validate data can greatly accelerate processes and reduce risk. In addition, establishing a dedicated data validation team during mock load or testing phases can significantly improve data quality and further minimize risks before production deployment.

11) Data Cleansing Approach: Data cleansing effort during migration depends primarily on two factors: the quality of the source system data and the number of years of history included in the migration scope. In top-of-stack migrations, effort is typically lower. However, when defining the migration scope, it is critical to recognize that expanding historical data coverage significantly increases cleansing complexity due to accumulated inconsistencies, missing standards, and legacy data issues.

12) Data Archival Strategy: Once you are live with SAP SuccessFactors HCM, we do not bring over all historical data. However, we still need historical data to support different requirements such as reporting, regulatory requirements, rehire processes, and tenure calculations..etc. The right data archival strategy must be defined based on the legacy HR system’s role after SuccessFactors goes live. Some partner tools provide seamless access to historical data from the SuccessFactors system.

13) System freeze strategy during Prod Loads: A proper strategy should be defined during production data loads, including how source system transactions will be governed during production loads. Depending on production load, timelines, and business impact, we carefully plan soft and hard freeze periods. We also define a delta migration approach to minimize disruption.

The InfoPorter tool supports a delta approach when migrating data from SAP S/4HANA HCM or SAP ERP HCM. If you are migrating data from other HCM systems, you should define the appropriate strategy based on system capabilities and project requirements

14) Production Load : Define production load timelines. We usually plan around three weeks to complete production load activities, including data load, validation, and sign-off. However, the timeline may vary depending on complexity. The goal is to achieve a 99.99% validation success rate, which serves as the exit criterion for production load completion.

 

Below is the High-Level SAP SuccessFactors Data Migration Framework cover all key areas in one picture :

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) Modernization Partner Demo Systems Reprovisioning
entry.content:
SAP is modernising all ECP Partner Demo Systems — moving from the older ERP HCM (ECC) stack to the powerful, future-ready S/4HANA HCM platform. This means faster performance, greater scalability, and a demo experience that truly reflects the innovation your customers expect.

What’s In It For You?

  • A brand-new, high-performing demo environment on S/4HANA HCM
  • Impress prospects with a modern platform that showcases ECP at its very best
  • Future-proof your demos with long-term SAP platform support
  • No disruption — your existing system stays live for a full 4 weeks while you transition

This is a reprovisioning, not a conversion — you’ll receive a fresh system with a new SID and URL, ready to go.

Your Transition Timeline at a Glance

PhaseWhat Happens
Request SubmittedSubmit via the SAP Partner Cloud Ops Portal
New System Goes LiveFresh S/4HANA HCM environment delivered
4-Week Overlap BeginsMigrate, configure, and validate — both systems run in parallel
Legacy System RetiredAutomatic decommissioning — your modern system takes centre stage

Here’s Your Fast-Track Checklist

Before you submit, make sure you’re set up for success:

  • Transports Ready — Workbench and Customizing transports prepared and S/4HANA compatible
  • Team Assigned — Functional and technical resources available to import, configure, and test
  • Plan Committed — All migration and validation activities completed within 1 month of go-live

How to Request Your New Demo System

  1. Log in to the SAP Partner Cloud Ops Portal using your partner credentials
  2. From the top navigation, select Support
  3. Locate and select ECP Partner Demo Reprovisioning
  4. Complete the request template in a PDF document and attach it to the ticket
  5. Ensure your submission aligns with your data center-specific timelines
  6. The Provisioning ticket to be created 10 days ahead of the scheduled date of your reprovisioning

After Your New System Is Live

Once your new S/4HANA HCM demo system is provisioned, raise a support ticket under the component LOD-EC-ECP-OPS and subject as “S4 Partner Demo Reprovisioning - Transport request - <SID>" to request :

  • Import of source system transports (Workbench, Customizing, and Add-ons where applicable)
  • Support for system migration and movement activities
  • Provide a full list of transports to be imported
  • Identify any network or integration considerations (e.g., SNC, interfaces, EC–ECP integrations)

Key Reminders

The legacy ERP HCM demo system will not be extended beyond the 4-week overlap period. Decommissioning is automatic — ensure all activities are completed on time.

Questions?

A webinar session was scheduled for June 9, 2026, from 14:00 to 15:00 UTC. You may listen in the recording for more details on this topic.
For any questions or clarifications, please reach out to the SAP ECP Modernization team at: SAP_SuccessFactors_ECP_Modernization@sap.com

We’re committed to making this transition as smooth as possible and look forward to supporting you through this modernization journey.

entry.title: 09.SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation - How to define Data Migration Scope
entry.content:
This is part of an ongoing blog series on SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation. In this post, we explore how to define the data migration scope and the critical factors to consider when finalizing it. The information and approach shared in this blog are based solely on my experience working on SAP SuccessFactors HCM projects over the last 12 years. 

Data migration is one of the most complex workstreams in an SAP SuccessFactors Implementation project. It is often the primary factor that determines whether an implementation is a resounding success or a mess.

To define an accurate scope, we use at least four sections to conduct a "deep dive" into customer-specific information. Based on these findings, we can define the final project scope:

 

  1. SF Module Scope: Which SAP SuccessFactors HCM modules are within the implementation scope?
  2. Historical Data: How much data history needs to be migrated from legacy HR systems?
  3. Countries in Scope: Which countries are included in this rollout?
  4. Migration Requirements: What are the high-level requirements for data migration?

1) SF Modules in Scope:        

  Identifying which SAP SuccessFactors HCM modules are in scope is the first step, and it is relatively straightforward. However, identifying which data objects need to be migrated for each module is critical. Not every module will necessarily require data migration, but evaluating all requirements and documenting them for every module will provide a clear picture of the project scope. Ultimately, this is one of the key metrics used to define the total level of effort

2) Data Migration History  : 

Deciding on the volume of historical data to migrate into SAP SuccessFactors HCM is a high-stakes balancing act. While the "Top of Stack"  is a leading practice recommendation, sometimes customer-specific requirements often demand more history. Based on my experience, the following 5 items are critical to define the historical data scope.

Here is a breakdown of how those five requirements influence your data migration strategy; the list may vary.

  • Historical and Legal Requirements: These are key driving factors in determining whether to migrate historical data. It is essential to document all country-specific requirements; based on these, a decision can be made regarding whether to migrate the data or maintain it within a legacy/archived system.
  • Compensation Module: To run effective merit cycles or bonus calculations, we typically require up to one year of data. The specific duration will vary based on the "go-live" date.
  • Rehire Requirements: Rehire policies often expand the scope to include the migration of terminated records. Depending on customer needs, you must identify how many years of terminated records are required for migration.
  • Retroactive (Retro) Requirements: Determining how far back the system must go to run retro-calculations is critical. Furthermore, the volume of retro-transactions is a decisive factor in whether migrating historical data is necessary.
  • Country-Specific Requirements: Local regulations significantly impact the decision to migrate history. It is mandatory to review all country-specific Reporting. legal and compliance requirements before finalizing the migration strategy.

 My Recommendation: I recommend doing  "Top of Stack" migration. If migrating historical data is absolutely necessary, it should be limited to a maximum of one year. Note that migrating history significantly increases project complexity. Specifically, the efforts required for data cleansing, validation, and loading will be substantially higher and must be factored into the effort, project timeline, and resource allocation.

3) Countries in scope and Source of data: 

Identifying all countries in scope is critical to the project’s success. As part of defining the geographic scope, identifying data sources is critical. If the HCM system is uniform across all countries, the impact on scope is minimal; however, a fragmented landscape of disparate systems will significantly increase project effort.

  • Translation Requirements: The need for multi-language data mapping and system localization, which requires additional logic in data migration.
  • Validation Complexity: Increased resource requirements for localized data cleansing and validation, which requires more effort.

 

Countries

In Scope

Population

Source HCM system

USA

Yes

28000 Active

SAP ERP HCM

Canada

Yes

12000 Active

SAP ERP HCM

UK

Yes

6500 Active

SAP ERP HCM

India

Yes

18000 Active

SAP ERP HCM

Australia

Yes

3400 Active

SAP ERP HCM

 4) Different Types of Employee Data Populations and Scenarios: 

Identifying different employee population classifications and their specific scenarios is critical, as it requires a tailored data migration approach, design, and logic that directly impact the total migration effort.

Once the discovery scope is complete, we define the project scope in a detailed document. As the first outcome-based deliverable for the data migration workstream, this report clarifies our strategic choices—such as prioritizing 'Top of Stack' data over full history. It also outlines unique customer requirements, etc. Based on this, we proposed a solution architecture for complex data migration requirements, tool selection for migration, cleansing, mock cycles planning, and a validation strategy.

Key Points to Remember:

  • Clearly distinguish between “must-have” and “nice-to-have” requirements (e.g., full historical data vs. top-of-stack data) to manage scope and complexity effectively.
  • Clearly document how to tackle customer-specific use cases when you migrate only the top of the stack. Eg: Retros, Historical Reporting, Rehire, Tenure etc. SI has to work with the customer to provide the right solution design based on customer-specific data (not a generic approach).
  • The success of data migration heavily depends on the expertise of your System Integrator (SI) partner's key resources. It is critical to onboard key data migration resources with proven experience.
  • Customer resource availability is essential at every stage. Assign dedicated team members who have a strong understanding of your data and business processes to actively support the project.
  • Assemble a team focused on execution. Data migration requires significant hands-on effort, so prioritize contributors who deliver outcomes over those who only provide input.

entry.title: How to update Username in SAP SuccessFactors - 1H 2026 update FAIL
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

After a very “character-building” 1H 2026 SuccessFactors update weekend — complete with a Latest People Profile RBP adventure related to Employee Views — we are continuing to discover additional surprises that somehow did not make themselves obvious in the “What’s New Viewer.” Think less “hidden feature” and more “new puppy surprise found in the dark with bare feet in the kitchen.”

Today’s episode is brought to you by KBA 2457516 — How to update Username in SAP SuccessFactors.

Prior to the upgrade, system administrators could directly update Basic/General Info within an associate’s profile. This was particularly useful in the occasional scenario where a newly onboarded associate received a generated username that conflicted with an inactive associate still sitting in SuccessFactors, despite already being purged from Azure.

The old process was beautifully simple:

  1. Attempt to assign the username to the new associate.
  2. Receive the very helpful message that the username already existed.
  3. Locate the inactive profile.
  4. Add an “X” to the end of the old username.
  5. Assign the intended username to the new associate.

Total elapsed time: about two minutes.
Stress level: peachy.
Application errors: mysteriously nonexistent.

Then came the 1H 2026 release, which politely informed us: “Absolutely not.”

Apparently, direct updates are now restricted due to the risk of “inconsistent behavior or application errors.” While I appreciate SAP’s concern for our wellbeing, I should note we had been performing this exact process for years without unleashing any known chaos into the system.

The new process now resembles an HRIT escape room challenge:

  • Download the UDF via Associate Export
  • Locate the active associate record
  • Build a Basic Import file
  • Navigate to Import Associate Data
  • Validate the file
  • Run the import
  • Wait for successful completion
  • Discover the username still did not update
  • Wonder briefly if reality itself is broken

After additional research, we then discover there is still an inactive associate using the generated username. So now:

  • Return to the UDF
  • Modify the inactive username with an “X” suffix
  • Rebuild the import file
  • Re-import using the “Process Inactive Associates” checkbox that absolutely cannot be forgotten
  • Wait for Scheduled Job Manager to bless the transaction
  • Rebuild and reimport the file again for the new associate
  • Refresh the profile repeatedly like a day trader watching stock prices

Finally… success.

Total elapsed time: approximately 15–20 minutes.
Application errors prevented: still apparently zero.
Administrative morale: under active investigation.

Apologies for the sarcasm, but after spending most of the weekend troubleshooting profile and permission issues tied to provisioning jobs and RBP migration steps that behaved differently in Production than Preview, my sense of humor is now functioning as a coping mechanism.

The good news is we are continuing to work through the issues.
The bad news is I now instinctively distrust any sentence beginning with “This enhancement improves the user experience.”

HAGD,

Garrett Meredith
Disgruntled System Admin, Esq.

entry.title: Replicating Custom MDF Data from SAP SuccessFactors EC to a Custom Infotype in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
I would like to share important integration steps for replicating employee data from a custom MDF in SAP SF EC to a custom infotype in SAP HCM S/4. Recently, we came across a business requirement to capture group share cost in percentages and replicate the data to S/4. The instructions were to handle it clean through SAP Standards, without doing any ABAP in the S/4.

Problem Statement:
When I started configuration, it was found that there is no standard portlet in EC to maintain such data so we had to create a custom portlet having custom fields accordingly. The replication was not getting successful, however, initially I was able to get fields in the Verbose Logs but the values were not getting replicated nor reflecting in the logs. Hence, I followed the following steps without making any enhancements in standard replication:

Prerequisites
Before starting, the following criteria is fulfilled:
• Connectivity between SAP SuccessFactors and SAP S/4HANA is established
• Standard employee replication working fine
• You have created a custom Infotype in SAP S/4HANA
• You have created a custom MDF object in SAP SuccessFactors EC

Decoupling the Infotype:
Before starting the configuration steps, it is important to understand the concept of Infotype decoupling, because it changes the entire integration approach. Decoupling an Infotype means separating it from direct database table operations. Instead of reading and writing data directly against the underlying database tables, all interactions happen through standard SAP APIs and framework classes. In practical terms, business logic, user interface, and data storage are managed independently of one another. In simple terms, the Infotype becomes framework-driven rather than table-driven.

Step-by-Step Configuration

Step 1: Create and Decouple the Custom Infotype in S/4HANA
Start by developing your custom Infotype in S/4HANA via ABAP. Once created, you need to add it in the decoupled Infotype table by maintaining the respective entry in table V_T582ITVCLAS using transaction SM30. This step is the foundation of the entire integration. If the Infotype is not decoupled here, none of the subsequent configuration will work correctly. The screenshot below shows the relevant SM30 entry.

Step 2: Create the Custom MDF Object in Employee Central
In SAP SuccessFactors EC, navigate to Configure Object Definition and create your custom MDF object.

Step 3: Define Custom Fields on the MDF Object
Add the required fields to your MDF object. Use the correct data types, for example, use Decimal for percentage values. Further, do not add “_” sign except the one after cust which is standard i.e cust_CustomField and not cust_Custom_Field to avoid any errors.

 

 

Step 4: Register the MDF Object in the Compound Employee API

For S/4HANA to be able to read your MDF data, you need to add the new object in the Compound Employee API. Navigate to: Manage Data ->  CompoundEmployeeObjectTypes ->  ObjectTypeList

Step 5: Define the Additional EC Entity in S/4HANA

Back in S/4HANA, create a new transformation template for your custom entity via the IMG. Follow this path:

SPRO -> SAP Reference IMG -> Personnel Management -> Integration with SuccessFactors Employee Central -> Business Integration Builder -> Data Replication from Employee Central -> Employee Data -> Field Mapping for Employee Data Replication from Employee Central -> Define Additional Employee Central Entities for Employee Data -> New Entry. When creating the entry, ensure you are maintaining the CF API Segment correctly i.e exactly the same as maintained in SAP SF EC. If wrongly maintained, your replication will be stopped and the employees will not go in the viewer screen.

Step 6: Import Metadata into S/4HANA
Export the data model file (XML) from EC using the Export Succession Data Model tool. Then, in S/4HANA, run the following program via SA38: ECPAO_ECTMPL_METADATA_WRITER
Provide your EC instance details and specify the newly created EC entity. This imports the MDF fields into S/4HANA and makes them available for field mapping. After the import, the fields will appear in table V_ECPAO_FLD, accessible via SM30.

Note: Fields can also be maintained manually in V_ECPAO_FLD if the import is not feasible. The End Date field (EC Field ID 99) must always be created manually, as it is not imported automatically.

Step 7: Define Field Mapping

When the metadata in uploaded successfully, you can now map EC MDF fields to the corresponding S/4HANA Infotype fields. Navigate to:

SPRO -> SAP Reference IMG -> Personnel Management -> Integration with SuccessFactors Employee Central -> Business Integration Builder -> Data Replication from Employee Central -> Employee Data -> Field Mapping for Employee Data Replication from Employee Central -> Define Field Mapping for Employee Data -> New Entry (or copy an existing Transformation Template)

Use Primary Mapping to connect each EC field to its corresponding Infotype field. 

Mistakes to avoid: When I was doing the configs, I was adding "_" in the SF Fields, or was not maintaining the fields in initials in capital letters e.g cust_customfield instead of cust_CustomField, and the most difficult one was not able to identify why the values are not getting replicated when fields are visible in the verbose log.  The compound employee API segment maintenance was missing or was wrongly maintained resulting in replication to stop.

Conclusion:
By combining a custom MDF object, the Compound Employee API, the Business Integration Builder, and a properly decoupled custom Infotype, we were able to deliver a solution that is clean, maintainable, and aligned with SAP’s best practices. No enhancements to standard replication were required.

SAP Community SAP SuccessFactors HCM Core SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite HCM (Human Capital Management) #SAP SF EC to SAP HCM S/4 Integration

entry.title: IAS for SuccessFactors Onboarding: Your Complete Migration Guide Before November 2026
entry.content:
The Deadline You Can't Ignore

If you're managing SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, November 13, 2026 needs to be on your calendar. That's when SAP will permanently remove basic authentication (username and password) for all SuccessFactors instances, including onboarding new hires.

This isn't a soft deadline. SAP ended maintenance on June 2, 2025. After November 2026, if you haven't migrated to Identity Authentication Service (IAS), your new hires won't be able to log in. Period.

Source: SAP KBA 3472405 and the official SAP Help Portal page "Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite". SAP KBA 2791410 also confirms: "IAS adoption will become mandatory by November, 2026."

The ones who started early had smooth rollouts. The ones who waited? Compressed timelines, production issues, and a lot of weekend work. This guide gives you the roadmap they wish they'd had.

What Is IAS and Why This Matters

SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) is SAP's cloud-based authentication platform. It's the future of access management across all SAP cloud solutions and part of SAP Cloud Identity Services alongside Identity Provisioning Service (IPS).

This migration isn't just about checking a compliance box. IAS brings capabilities you can't get with basic authentication:

Security That Meets Modern Standards
Multi-factor authentication, risk-based authentication, centralized password policies. Basic authentication has well-documented security vulnerabilities, and regulatory scrutiny around credential management keeps increasing.

Unified Access Across SAP Solutions
Authenticate once, access everything. SuccessFactors, SAP Analytics Cloud for Story Reports, other SAP solutions all integrate through IAS. No more juggling multiple credential sets.

Future-Ready Architecture
Story Reports in People Analytics already require IAS. New capabilities like Joule AI and advanced analytics follow the same pattern. SAP's product roadmap assumes you have IAS.

Real-Time Provisioning
With the SCIM API and real-time sync, new hires receive credentials immediately when onboarding begins, not after a scheduled batch job runs hours later.

Understand Your Starting Point: The Three Integration Types

SAP has three distinct integration patterns. Your migration complexity depends entirely on which one applies to you.

The Type 1 / Type 2 / Type 3 framework used below is not original to this guide. It comes from the SAP-published community blog "Onboarding New Hires Authentication using SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)" on the SAP Community (under Human Capital Management Blog Posts by SAP ).

That post is the canonical reference and you should read it in full. The summary here exists only to orient you before you do.

Type 1: Newly Provisioned Instances (Post-December 9, 2022)

If your SuccessFactors instance was provisioned after December 9, 2022, you're already on SCIM API. The backend integration happened automatically during provisioning.

How to verify: Navigate to Provisioning > Company Settings. Look for the "Onboardee Identity Authentication" switch. If it's enabled and grayed out, you're Type 1.

What this means: Your IAS/IPS infrastructure is ready. Your focus is configuration (email templates, real-time sync setup, and testing).

Type 2: Existing Instances with IAS Upgrade After December 2022

If you initiated an IAS upgrade through the Upgrade Center after December 9, 2022, you're also on SCIM.

How to verify: Navigate to Admin Center > Monitoring Tool for Identity Authentication/Identity Provisioning Service Upgrade > Settings tab. If you see "Employee and Onboardee Application Completed" (grayed out), you're Type 2.

What this means: Like Type 1, your backend is SCIM-ready. Configuration and testing are your focus areas.

Type 3: Legacy ODATA Connector (Pre-December 2022 IAS Integration)

If you integrated IAS before December 9, 2022, you're on the legacy ODATA connector. This is the most complex scenario because you must upgrade from ODATA to SCIM before enabling onboarding authentication.

How to verify: Log into Identity Provisioning Service > Source Systems > Select your SuccessFactors source > Properties. Look for sf.api.version. Version 1 = ODATA. Version 2 = SCIM.

What this means: You have a two-phase migration. First upgrade ODATA to SCIM, then configure onboarding authentication. Budget 3-5 additional weeks for this.

Critical Limitations for ODATA Connectors:

  • Onboarding new hires sync via scheduled IPS jobs only (no real-time sync available)
  • New hires receive IAS activation email only after the IPS job runs
  • Cancelled onboarding accounts deactivate only when the next IPS job runs
  • Rehires require manual workarounds (deleting old email addresses)

This is why SAP strongly recommends migrating to SCIM if you're still on ODATA. Budget 3–5 additional weeks for the ODATA→SCIM upgrade before you start the onboarding configuration.

Pre-Migration Planning: Answer These Questions First

1. Do You Have Corporate SSO for Employees?

If employees authenticate through Azure AD, Okta, Ping Identity, or another corporate IdP, you need conditional authentication in IAS so that employees route to your corporate IdP while pre-day-1 onboardees use IAS password authentication.

Without this: You'll force new hires to authenticate through your corporate directory before they have corporate accounts. The classic chicken-and-egg problem.

Reference: SAP KBA 2954556 "How to implement Partial SSO after Identity Authentication implementation on SuccessFactors" covers conditional authentication patterns. 

2. What's Your Rehire Strategy?

Rehires are the most common source of production issues. Here's what should happen:

  1. Employee terminates from Employee Central
  2. Their IAS account gets deleted (not just deactivated). Set property: ips.delete.existedbefore.entities=true
  3. When rehired through onboarding, a new IAS account is created
  4. New account links to their reactivated Employee Central record
  5. On hire date, their account transitions from "onboardee" to "employee" type

Source for this guidance: SAP Community blog "Considerations when implementing IAS for SuccessFactors Onboarding" (December 2025, post ba-p/14288604). The blog lays out the rehire flow and recommends the ips.delete.existedbefore.entities=true setting explicitly. The behavior of this property is also documented in SAP KBA 3017663 and KBA 3405942.

Test this flow extensively. We've seen multiple go-lives where rehire workflows failed because old IAS accounts weren't properly deleted.

3. Are You Still on Onboarding 1.0?

"SAP is extending the End of Maintenance date from the Second Half 2025 Release to the First Half 2026 Release. This means that Onboarding 1.0 will now receive compliance updates and security patches through the 1H 2026 Release."

Practically, that means:

  • End of Maintenance: May 14, 2026 (1H 2026 release)
  • Deletion: 1H 2026 Production release — Onboarding 1.0 and its data become inaccessible
  • There is no automated migration tool from ONB 1.0 to the new Onboarding solution; it's a reimplementation.

Decision point: Should you migrate to the new Onboarding first, then implement IAS? Or implement IAS on ONB 1.0, then migrate?

If you're within 6 months of planning a new Onboarding migration, do that first. Implementing IAS twice (once on ONB 1.0, again on the new Onboarding) creates unnecessary complexity and risk.

Note: ONB 1.0 treats pre-day-1 users as "active employees," so they sync through standard IPS jobs. The new Onboarding (formerly "ONB 2.0") treats them as external users with the pm_product_name=ONB URL parameter handled differently.

4. Do You Have Learning-Only External Users?

External learning users (partners, contractors, customers) maintain separate IAS accounts from onboardees. Each requires a unique personal email address and username.

Naming convention matters. Plan how you'll distinguish external learners from onboardees to avoid account collisions.

5. What's Your Change Window?

IAS migration requires a service interruption. You cannot switch authentication methods seamlessly.

Best practices:

  • Schedule during a period with no active onboarding processes
  • Weekend or holiday window preferred
  • Budget 4-6 hours for production migration (even if preview took 2 hours)
  • Validate that all in-flight onboarding cases are completed or paused before starting

The 6-Phase Migration Strategy

Phase 1: Initiate IAS Integration via Upgrade Center

The phase structure below is a synthesis of the official SAP Help guide "Setting up SAP Identity Authentication Service for New Hires Using System for Cross-domain Identity Management (SCIM) API", SAP KBA 2791410 (the master implementation guide), Jaideep Shetty's three-part "IAS for ONB2.0 New Hires" SAP Community series (March 2023, posts ba-p/13549508, ba-p/13549763, and the transformations post on Technology Blog Posts by Members), and the series "IPS to IAS migration".

Prerequisites:

  • Upgrade Center access permission
  • Customer S-User credentials (Partner S-Users cannot trigger this upgrade)
  • If you already have an IAS tenant: URL and admin access
  • Verify existing IAS tenants at https://iamtenants.accounts.cloud.sap/

Procedure:

Step 1: Navigate to Upgrade Center
Admin Center > Upgrade Center > Search for "Initiate the SAP Cloud Identity Services Identity Authentication Service Integration"

Step 2: Authenticate with S-User
Enter the S-User credentials you use to open support cases. If authentication fails, check SAP KBA 2944990 for common issues.

Step 3: Select or Create IAS Tenant
You'll see a list of available IAS tenants. Since 2H 2025, the list shows an "Ownership" field:

  • Owned: Registered under the same customer ID as your SuccessFactors system
  • Trusted: Belongs to a different customer ID but accessible via trust relationship

Decision time: Use existing tenant or create new?

  • 1 IAS per 1 SF approach: More IAS tenants to manage, more applications on corporate IdP, but simpler user management
  • Shared IAS approach: Fewer tenants to manage, but you need to handle user matching between instances and customize IPS transformation rules

Step 4: Submit and Wait
The process takes 2+ hours. You'll receive an email with IAS admin credentials if it's a new tenant.

Important: For upgrades after 2H 2022, the IPS-to-SF connection uses certificate-based authentication (mTLS). The legacy IPSADMIN user is no longer created. This is documented in KBA 2791410 and discussed in the  post "Real-Time Sync of New Hires from SAP SuccessFactors to IAS".

Phase 2: Configure IPS (Identity Provisioning Service)

Prerequisites:

  • Completed Phase 1
  • Admin access to IPS tenant (verify at https://iamtenants.accounts.cloud.sap/)

Critical Configuration Steps:

Configure Source System Properties (SuccessFactors)

Navigate to IPS > Source Systems > [Your SF System] > Properties

Required properties for SCIM (sf.api.version = 2):

Property Value Purpose
sf.user.filteractive eq 'true'Syncs all active users including onboardees
sf.api.version2SCIM API (version 1 = legacy ODATA)
ips.delete.existedbefore.entitiestrueProperly handles rehires by deleting old accounts

Source for filter syntax under SCIM v2:  "Onboardees are not replicated into IAS through the scheduled Job": "If you have already migrated to the SCIM connector, then the only possible filter is sf.user.filter=active eq 'true'." SAP KBA 3464278 also documents which attributes can be used for sf.user.filter when sf.api.version=2.

For legacy ODATA connectors (sf.api.version = 1):

The filter looks different for ODATA:

sf.user.filter = status in 'active','active_external_suite' and (personKeyNav/userAccountNav/userType in 'employee', 'onboardee')

Configure Source System Transformation (ODATA only)

If you're on ODATA, update the transformation JSON to distinguish employee vs onboardee. Navigate to IPS > Source Systems > [Your SF System] > Transformation and add:

{
    "condition": "($.status == 't') && ($.personKeyNav.userAccountNav.userType == 'employee')",
    "constant": "employee",
    "targetPath": "$.userType"
},
{
    "condition": "($.status == 'active_external_suite') && ($.personKeyNav.userAccountNav.userType == 'onboardee')",
    "constant": "onboardee2.0",
    "targetPath": "$.userType"
}

Configure Target System Transformation (IAS)

Navigate to IPS > Target Systems > [Your IAS System] > Transformation

Add this fragment to map onboardee user types:

{
    "condition": "$.userType contains 'onboardee'",
    "constant": "Public",
    "targetPath": "$.userType"
}
 
Configure Email Template Set ID

You need to tell IPS which IAS email template to use for onboardee activation emails.

First, find your template set ID:

  • Log into IAS Admin Console
  • Navigate to Email Templates > SF ONB 2.0 On-Behalf Registration
  • Click on the template
  • Copy the UUID from the URL (it's the long string after "Template Sets")

Then add to IPS Target System Transformation:

{
    "condition": "$.userType == 'onboardee2.0'",
    "constant": "c33e67c2-2c03-452f-86d7-7b40be5af9d4",
    "targetPath": "$.emailTemplateSetId",
    "scope": "createEntity"
}

(Replace the constant value with your actual template set ID)

Source: Jaideep's "IAS for ONB2.0 New Hires – 3 (Transformations)" SAP Community post documents this exact pattern for separating onboardee vs. employee email templates. The approach also appears in SAP Community Q&A "IAS + ONB (Welcome Email Day 1)" (post qaq-p/14042943).

Configure SendMail Behavior

This is critical. These fragments control when IAS sends activation emails. Add to IPS Target System Transformation:

{ "constant": "false", "targetPath": "$.sendMail", "scope": "createEntity" },
{ "condition": "$.userType == 'onboardee2.0'", "constant": "true", "targetPath": "$.sendMail", "scope": "createEntity" },
{ "constant": "true", "targetPath": "$.mailVerified", "scope": "createEntity" },
{ "condition": "$.userType == 'onboardee2.0'", "constant": "false", "targetPath": "$.mailVerified", "scope": "createEntity" },
{ "constant": "disabled", "targetPath": "$.passwordStatus", "scope": "createEntity" },
{ "condition": "$.userType == 'employee'", "constant": "enabled", "targetPath": "$.passwordStatus", "scope": "createEntity" }

What this does:

  • Onboardees receive IAS activation emails
  • Employees don't receive activation emails (they use corporate SSO)
  • Onboardee accounts start with email unverified, forcing them to verify via the activation link

Schedule IPS Sync Job

Navigate to IPS > Jobs > Run Now (for testing) or set up a schedule.

Verify sync is working: IPS > Job Logs > Review last execution for read/write counts and errors.

Phase 3: Enable Real-Time Sync (SCIM Only)

Real-time sync means new hire accounts are created in IAS immediately when onboarding begins, not after a scheduled job runs hours later.

Source: The detailed real-time sync setup is documented in the SAP Help Portal page "Manage Real-Time Sync of New Hires from SAP SuccessFactors to Identity Authentication with Identity Provisioning" and in the post "Real-Time Sync of New Hires from SAP SuccessFactors to IAS > 3 of 4". Jaideep Shetty's "IAS for ONB2.0 New Hires – 2" covers the same ground from a different angle.

Prerequisites:

  • SCIM connector (sf.api.version = 2)
  • Admin access to both SuccessFactors and IAS

Procedure:

Generate X.509 Certificate in SuccessFactors

Admin Center > Security > Certificate Management > Generate new certificate

Important: Leave the "Login Name" field empty. A technical user in the backend handles permissions. If you populate this field, you can run into permission issues later.

Create System User in IAS

IAS Administration Console > Users > Create system user > Upload the X.509 certificate > Grant "Provisioning" permissions

Register Certificate in Integration Service

SuccessFactors > Integration Service Registration Center > Register the certificate

Destination URL format:

https://<ips-tenant-url>/ipsproxy/service/api/v1/systems/<source-system-id>

You can get the source-system-id from the IPS URL when you click on your source system. It's in the browser address bar.

Enable Real-Time Sync

Admin Center > Manage Data > Manage Identity Provisioning Real Time Sync > Enable for Onboardee

Verify it's working: Create a test onboarding case. Check Admin Center > Execution Manager > Pre-delivered Integration dashboard. You should see real-time sync events appearing.

Phase 4: Configure Email Templates

You have two approaches here. I've seen both work, but Option A tends to reduce support tickets.

Option A: Dual Email Flow (Recommended)

Send a welcome email from SuccessFactors explaining the process, followed immediately by the IAS activation email.

Attribution: This dual-email recommendation comes from SAP Community blog "Considerations when implementing IAS for SuccessFactors Onboarding" (post ba-p/14288604).

SuccessFactors Configuration:

  1. Keep the (ONB) External User Welcome Message template active
  2. Remove the password reset link token from the template (this is critical)
  3. Add clear instructions that look something like this:
Welcome to [Company]! Your onboarding journey begins here.

You will receive a second email from SAP Cloud Identity Services within the next few minutes with your login credentials. Please wait for that email to access the onboarding system.

If you don't receive the activation email within 10 minutes, please contact [HR contact email/phone].
  1. Translate this message into all your supported languages

IAS Configuration: Configure the activation email template in IAS Admin Console > Applications > SuccessFactors Application > Email Templates

Why this works: The first email sets expectations and provides context. The second email provides the actual credentials. Users understand it's a two-step process instead of being confused about which email to use.

Option B: Single Email from IAS

Disable SuccessFactors welcome emails entirely. New hires receive only the IAS activation email.

SuccessFactors Configuration:

  1. Navigate to Email Notification Templates
  2. Disable these templates:
    • (ONB) External User Welcome Message
    • (ONB) Rehire User Welcome Message

The recommendation to disable these specific templates if going single-email comes from the SAP Community blog "Onboarding New Hires Authentication using SAP IAS", which states: "It is recommended to Disable the following templates in Email Services: Template: (ONB) External User Welcome Message Template, Template: (ONB) Rehire User Welcome Message Template."

IAS Configuration: Customize the IAS activation email to include onboarding context, not just login instructions.

Tradeoff: Simpler (one email instead of two), but less context for the new hire. They receive a generic IAS email without understanding it's part of their onboarding journey.

Update Start Onboarding Template

Regardless of which option you chose, edit the (ONB) Start Onboarding process template to include:

Before proceeding, ensure you have activated your account using the activation email from SAP Cloud Identity Services.

If you have not received the activation email, please contact your HR administrator at [contact details].

Add this message in all your supported languages.

Phase 5: Configure IAS Application Settings

Set Home URL Redirect

IAS Admin Console > Applications > SuccessFactors Application > Authentication and Access > Home URL

Set it to:

https://<datacenter>.successfactors.com/login?company=<CompanyID>
